content:
Hi,

There are some similar questions about the contribution process but I thought this warranted its own topic:
https://rust-for-linux.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/291565-Help/topic/Review.20process
https://rust-for-linux.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/291565-Help/topic/.E2.9C.94.20What's.20the.20process.20of.20sending.20a.20PR.3F

I have an issue and (very WIP) draft PR for changes that (we think) are needed to support the android binder driver in rust. I don't have access to assign myself to the issue or add reviewers to the PR (specifically I'd like to cc @Alice Ryhl). Is that expected?
FWIW, I just tried if I can add Alice as a reviewer, and Github didn't ask for confirmation, so the addition is taking effect, sorry..
I was about to say that for pinging people, so far we used the @ syntax. We haven't really used the assign-to features in GitHub so far.
(But I assigned the issue to you Matt nevertheless)
Maybe the problem Matt has is because he is not a member of the Rust-for-Linux Github org
I think the @ syntax works for everybody, I will check what the requirements are for the other features. Added Matt in case it helps anyway.
Thanks! I'm still not able to request other reviewers for some reason (that's fine for now - I was just trying to add Alice). It does appear I can @ her in the issue now.
I think you can always @ -- the UI may not show the name if they are not a member, but you can still type it manually, and as far as I understand, the notification arrives.

Matt Gilbride said:

Thanks! I'm still not able to request other reviewers for some reason (that's fine for now - I was just trying to add Alice). It does appear I can @ her in the issue now.

Not sure what we decided for process? I thought we decided to use the mailing list? Send the patches as RFC and CC people?

Yeah, that is correct (the issue list is used, though, and it is the one we declare in MAINTAINERS). The PRs may still be helpful for others to do early reviews or run an easy CI before hitting the mailing list. We will see what is most useful there, after most things are upstreamed, and how people feel about it. We can always just disable them at some point (i.e. have a bot closing them and giving a useful message, since I think GitHub still does not allow to actually disable them).
